does: This paper introduces Acoustic Interference Attack (AIA), a novel jailbreak method against Large Audio Language Models that exploits acoustic latent semantics—paralinguistic features learned by audio generative models—rather than embedding malicious content directly into audio signals. The key contribution is demonstrating that safety alignment can be compromised by benign audio infused with specific acoustic latent semantics, decoupling the attack payload from the audio carrier itself. The authors reveal that interference audio with particular underlying acoustic properties acts as a universal jailbreak trigger, bypassing existing defenses that assume harmful content must be explicitly encoded in audio parameters or semantic optimization.
connects: This work builds on recent jailbreak research for large language and multimodal models, extending attack paradigms from text and vision modalities to audio. It responds to the growing integration of audio into large language models and the expanding attack surface this creates. Rather than following existing approaches that treat audio as a vessel for malicious payloads through acoustic parameter control, semantic optimization, or additive perturbation, this paper draws on understanding of latent representations in generative models and safety alignment mechanisms. The research connects to prior work examining how models learn paralinguistic features and how their alignment can be vulnerable to subtle distributional shifts, applying these insights specifically to the audio modality.
enables: This work opens new research directions in understanding safety vulnerabilities in multimodal models, particularly how alignment mechanisms in one modality interact with latent representations from another. It could spur development of more robust defenses that account for acoustic latent semantics rather than just explicit content filtering. Future work might explore similar interference-based attacks across other modalities, examine whether the universal properties of these acoustic triggers can be theoretically characterized, and investigate how to design audio models with inherent robustness to such latent-level interference. The findings also prompt deeper investigation into what aspects of audio generative model priors create these vulnerabilities and how alignment training can be made resistant to attacks that exploit learned paralinguistic structure.
